sourceRef.citation: Erandi Sachinthanie Imiya Mudiyanselage et al. 2026

statement.en: Flagellar Coordination in a Swimming Multicellular Bacterium
statement.summary: Microbial locomotion is well understood when cells use either a small number of flagella or a high density of cilia. However many microbes live in an intermediate regime where coordination strategies such as flagellar bundles and metachronal waves are not possible. The mechanisms by which such organisms coordinate the motion of their swimming appendages are not well understood. Here, we study the only known obligatory multicellular bacterium, which are of the genus Magnetoglobus. Cells of this genus live exclusively in spherical communities called consortia, which are composed of a monolayer of tens of cells. Approximately a thousand of flagella project outward from the surface of each consortium, enabling swimming at speeds up to 190 μm/s. We reconstruct the velocity field around the swim

proposer: Erandi Sachinthanie Imiya Mudiyanselage, Melina Mati, Alexander P. Petroff

reiTyping.dfumt8Reason: This is a preprint (arXiv timestamp 2026-04-25, posted to cs.AI category) describing empirical observations of flagellar coordination in Magnetoglobus consortia. The abstract presents experimental reconstruction of hydrodynamic fields but appears incomplete ('enabling swimming at speeds up to 190 μm/s. We reconstruct the velocity field around the swim...'). The underlying biological phenomenon exists (TRUE ontologically), but the paper's specific claims about coordination mechanisms remain under peer review and validation. Classified as FLOWING rather than TRUE because: (1) preprint status, (2) incomplete abstract suggests ongoing work, (3) coordination mechanisms in this regime are stated as 'not well understood,' indicating provisional knowledge contribution.

ramanujanFormula.interpretation: 個々の鞭毛の位相が集団速度へと自発的に編み込まれる。
